title: Deep Learning by Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville: 9780262035613 | PenguinRandomHouse.com: Books
excerpts:
Section Title: Deep Learning > By Ian Goodfellow , Yoshua Bengio and Aaron Courville > Book Description
Content:
The text offers mathematical and conceptual background, covering relevant concepts in linear algebra, probability theory and information theory, numerical computation, and machine learning. It describes deep learning techniques used by practitioners in industry, including deep feedforward networks, regularization, optimization algorithms, convolutional networks, sequence modeling, and practical methodology; and it surveys such applications as natural language processing, speech recognition, computer vision, online recommendation systems, bioinformatics, and videogames. Finally, the book offers research perspectives, covering such theoretical topics as linear factor models, autoencoders, representation learning, structured probabilistic models, Monte Carlo methods, the partition function, approximate inference, and deep generative models.

title: Yoshua Bengio: The Past, Present, and Future of Deep Learning
excerpts:
Section Title:  > Yoshua Bengio: The Past, Present, and Future of Deep Learning
Content:
Professor Bengio is a Full Professor at the [Université de Montréal](https://www.umontreal.ca/) as well as Founder and Scientific Director of the [MILA-Quebec AI Institute](https://mila.quebec/en/) and the [IVADO institute](https://ivado.ca/en/) . Best known for his work in pioneering deep learning, Bengio was [one of three awardees](https://awards.acm.org/about/2018-turing) of the 2018 A.M. Turing Award along with [Geoffrey Hinton](https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~hinton/) and [Yann LeCun](http://yann.lecun.com/) . He is also the awardee of the prestigious [Killam prize](https://canadacouncil.ca/funding/prizes/killam-prizes) and, as of this year, the computer scientist with the [highest h-index in the world](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=kukA0LcAAAAJ&hl=en) .

title: Yoshua Bengio on why AI can behave unpredictably (and ...
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > When AI behaviour becomes hard to predict
Content:
In 2018, Bengio was awarded the Turing Award, the so-called 'Nobel Prize of computing', which he shared with fellow Godfathers of AI, Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun, both of whom were career mentors.
At the turn of the Millennium, his landmark paper " [A Neural Probabilistic Language Model](https://amturing.acm.org/award_winners/bengio_3406375.cfm) " tackled a core challenge in getting computers to understand human language: the sheer number of possible word combinations makes training AI systems enormously difficult.
His solution – word embeddings – gave networks a way to represent the *meaning* of words mathematically, so they could recognize when different phrases convey the same idea, even if the exact words differ. This breakthrough has since transformed how machines translate and understand language.

Section Title: ... > [Kate Whiting](https://www.weforum.org/stories/authors/kate-whiting/)
Content:
Senior Writer, Forum Stories
Yoshua Bengio is a Professor of Computer Science at the University of Montreal and one of the 'Godfathers of AI' alongside his mentors Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun.
At the World Economic Forum Annual Meeting in January, he sat down with Radio Davos to explain why AI can behave badly – and his solution.
In this edited interview, Bengio explains the lightbulb moment that spurred him to work on an AI system with no hidden agenda.
In early 2023, like many people, Yoshua Bengio was experimenting with ChatGPT. The Canadian computer scientist is widely considered one of the 'Godfathers of AI' for his pioneering work on neural networks and deep learning, so when he engages with these systems, he does so with a deep understanding of how they work.

title: Yoshua Bengio Doesn't Think We… - Apple Podcasts
excerpts:
Section Title: Yoshua Bengio Doesn’t Think We’re Ready for Superhuman AI. We’re Building it Anyway.
Content:
Bengio is one of AI’s pioneering figures. In 2018, he and two colleagues won the Turing Award – the closest thing computer science has to a Nobel Prize – for their work on deep learning. In 2022, he was the most cited computer scientist in the world. It wouldn’t be hyperbolic to suggest that AI as we know it today might not exist without Yoshua Bengio.
But in the last couple of years, Bengio has had an epiphany of sorts. And he now believes that, left unchecked, AI has the potential to wipe out humanity. So these days, he’s dedicated himself to AI safety. He’s a professor at the University of Montreal and the founder of MILA - the Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute.
And he was at this big AI conference too, amidst all these Silicon Valley types, pleading with the industry to slow down before it’s too late.

title: Yoshua Bengio joins hundreds of signatories in open letter ...
excerpts:
Section Title: Yoshua Bengio joins hundreds of signatories in open letter pleading for safer AI systems
Content:
March 31, 2023
Hundreds of key players in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) and technology, including Yoshua Bengio, Founder and Scientific Director of [Mila](https://mila.quebec/en/) - Quebec AI Institute co-signed on March 29, 2023  an open letter initiated by the [Future of Life Institute](https://futureoflife.org) to ask AI labs to immediately halt training of AI systems more powerful than GPT-4 for at least six months.
The open letter, titled “Pause Giant AI Experiments”, states that AI systems with intelligence that can rival human intelligence can pose serious threats to society and humanity, and that “recent months have seen AI labs locked in an out-of-control race to develop and deploy ever more powerful digital minds that no one – not even their creators – can understand, predict, or reliably control.”

title: Yoshua Bengio
excerpts:
Section Title: Yoshua Bengio > 2018 ACM A.M. Turing Award
Content:
Improving backpropagation algorithms : LeCun proposed an early version of the backpropagation algorithm (backprop), and gave a clean derivation of it based on variational principles. His work to speed up backpropagation algorithms included describing two simple methods to accelerate learning time.

title: Yoshua Bengio: Home
excerpts:
Section Title: Home > A.M. Turing Award Recipient and most-cited AI researcher
Content:
**Yoshua Bengio** is Full Professor of Computer Science at [Université de Montreal](https://www.umontreal.ca/) , Co-President and Scientific Director of [LawZero](http://lawzero.org/) , as well as the Founder and Scientific Advisor of [Mila](http://mila.quebec/) . He also holds a Canada [CIFAR](https://cifar.ca/) AI Chair. Considered one of the world’s leaders in Artificial Intelligence and Deep Learning, he is the recipient of the 2018 A.M. Turing Award, considered to be the “Nobel Prize of computing.” He is the most cited computer scientist worldwide, and the most-cited living scientist across all fields (by total citations).
